subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Neutrinos: Ghost Particles
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Neutrinos: Ghost Particles.

hook.text: Neutrinos are nearly massless particles that pass through everything. Billions fly through your body every second unnoticed.

buildup.text: They're produced in nuclear reactions—the Sun, supernovae, and nuclear reactors all emit enormous numbers of them.

discovery.text: In 1987, detectors caught 24 neutrinos from a supernova in the Large Magellanic Cloud—the first from outside our solar system.

twist.text: Neutrinos can escape places light can't. They fly straight out of a supernova's core while light takes hours to reach the surface.

climax.text: IceCube, buried in Antarctic ice, detects high-energy neutrinos from distant blazars and other cosmic accelerators.

punchline.text: They pass through planets without stopping. Ghost messengers from the most violent events.

quiz.question: Why are neutrinos useful for studying supernovae?
options: They escape the core instantly while light takes hours, providing early information
They are the only particles produced during a supernova explosion
They carry more energy than any other particle from stellar explosions
